structured_choices.data_source: 1000 Genomes Project public repository: 200,000 samples with genome-wide SNP data and disease phenotype labels. Data spans 10 years of collection. We will use only samples with complete SNP genotype calls (>99% call rate) and remove samples with documented non-European ancestry outliers via PCA to establish a consistent population base for initial comparison, noting this as a scope limitation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary disease susceptibility label derived from family history + genetic marker thresholds. A sample is labeled positive if: (a) documented family history of disease in first-degree relatives AND (b) presence of ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS. Negative label: no family history and <2 disease-associated SNPs. Samples in ambiguous middle range are excluded to avoid soft labels and label noise. This threshold-based approach is transparent and reproducible, though we acknowledge it is a proxy for true susceptibility and may introduce classification noise.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split within each ancestry/population subgroup. First, we stratify by self-reported population (European, African, Asian, admixed) to ensure each split carries similar population composition. Within each stratum, we randomly split 70% train / 15% validation / 15% test. This approach mitigates population stratification bias (a known confounder in genomic prediction) by ensuring the model is not trained on one population and tested on another. A temporal split is not appropriate here because SNP data are static and samples lack a meaningful temporal ordering; random stratified split is the correct choice.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (SNP filtering, imputation, normalization) are fit exclusively on the training set. Allele frequency filtering thresholds, missing data imputation parameters, and z-score normalization constants are computed from training data only, then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage from future data into the training pipeline.
structured_choices.features: Raw features: 500,000+ SNPs from the 1000 Genomes genotype data. Preprocessing: (1) Remove SNPs with minor allele frequency (MAF) < 5% in the training set to reduce noise and computational burden. (2) Impute missing genotypes using mean imputation within each population stratum (fitted on training set only). (3) Encode genotypes as allele counts (0, 1, 2). (4) Apply z-score normalization (mean=0, std=1) per SNP, fit on training set. Final feature count: ~50,000–100,000 SNPs. No hand-crafted interaction features or pathway-based aggregation; the transformer is expected to learn relevant SNP patterns. This high-dimensional feature set with >1000× more features than samples is a deliberate test of the deep learning model's ability to avoid overfitting.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based model: A bidirectional transformer encoder (e.g., BERT-like architecture adapted for genomics, or a Vision Transformer repurposed for sequence embeddings) with ~2–4 transformer blocks, 8 attention heads, hidden dimension 256, followed by a global pooling layer and 2-layer MLP classifier (hidden: 128, output: 1 sigmoid). The model processes genomic sequences as tokens (SNPs) and learns contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants. Total parameters: ~10–15 million. Regularization: dropout (0.3), L2 weight decay (1e-4), early stopping on validation AUC. Training uses Adam optimizer, learning rate 1e-4 with cosine annealing, batch size 32, up to 100 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression with L2 regularization trained on the same 50,000–100,000 SNP features (after identical preprocessing fit on training set). The logistic regression is tuned with equivalent effort: grid search over regularization strength (C: [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]) using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, selecting C that maximizes validation AUC. This baseline is fair because it uses the same feature set, undergoes comparable hyperparameter tuning, and is evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions. Logistic regression is the standard statistical model for genomic susceptibility prediction and represents the current state-of-practice that the transformer must outperform.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) computed on the held-out test set. Justification: (1) AUC-ROC is threshold-independent and appropriate for disease susceptibility prediction where the clinical operating point (sensitivity vs. specificity trade-off) may vary by deployment context. (2) It directly addresses the stakeholder objective of 'improving accuracy' and is standard in genomic prediction literature. (3) It is robust to class imbalance (though we will also report class distributions). (4) AUC-ROC captures the model's ability to rank positive cases higher than negative cases, which is essential for stratifying patients into risk tiers.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC (to assess performance at high recall if clinical deployment prioritizes sensitivity)
Balanced accuracy (TP rate + TN rate) / 2, to ensure the model does not achieve high AUC by heavily biasing toward one class
Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. empirical frequency in test set deciles) to assess whether predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for clinical use
Per-population AUC: report AUC separately for each ancestry group to detect whether the transformer exhibits population-specific performance gaps
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping use the validation set (15% of data). The validation set is used once per fold during cross-validation grid search for the baseline, and for early stopping (monitoring validation AUC) during transformer training. The test set (remaining 15%) is held out and touched only at the very end, after all hyperparameter choices and model architecture decisions are finalized. No data leakage from test to validation to training.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is reserved for a single evaluation pass at the end of the experiment. After model selection is complete (transformer and baseline both tuned on training + validation), both models are applied to the test set exactly once. Results (primary and secondary metrics) are computed and reported. The test set is never used for hyperparameter optimization, model selection, or any intermediate decision-making.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Population stratification (primary confound): Addressed by stratified random splitting and per-population AUC reporting. By ensuring each ancestry group appears proportionally in train/val/test, we prevent the model from learning spurious ancestry-phenotype correlations. Environmental/lifestyle confounds: The 1000 Genomes Project provides limited environmental metadata; we cannot fully control for lifestyle factors at present. However, we acknowledge this limitation and note that the current design measures genetic prediction in isolation. If environmental data becomes available, future work should include these covariates in both the baseline logistic regression and as contextual embeddings in the transformer. Overfitting due to high dimensionality: Addressed by strong regularization (dropout 0.3, L2 0.0001, early stopping), and by validating on a held-out set. We also report training vs. validation AUC curves to detect overfitting. If divergence occurs, the model is penalized in model selection. Label noise: By using strict, transparent label criteria and excluding ambiguous samples, we reduce label noise. We also perform a sensitivity analysis: retest the final model with relaxed label thresholds on the test set to assess robustness to label definition choices.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Disease Susceptibility Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We will use genomic sequence data from the 1000 Genomes Project, comprising approximately 200,000 samples with genome-wide SNP genotypes and disease phenotype labels. To establish a methodologically sound baseline, we will restrict the initial analysis to samples with >99% SNP call rate and consistent population ancestry (identified via principal component analysis). Samples will be assigned a binary disease susceptibility label using a transparent, reproducible criterion: positive label if the sample has documented family history of disease in first-degree relatives AND carries ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from published genome-wide association studies (GWAS). Negative label if no family history AND <2 disease SNPs. Samples in the ambiguous middle range will be excluded to minimize label noise and ensure clear separation. This approach acknowledges that our label is a proxy for true susceptibility; sensitivity analyses will later validate robustness to threshold changes.

### 2. Data Splits: Stratified Random Approach

Population stratification is a known major confounder in genomic prediction—models can spuriously learn ancestry rather than disease biology. To mitigate this, we employ a **stratified random split**. First, samples are stratified by self-reported ancestry/population (European, African, Asian, admixed). Within each population stratum, we randomly allocate 70% to training, 15% to validation, and 15% to test. This ensures each set contains proportional representation of all ancestries, preventing the model from learning ancestry-phenotype confounds. A temporal split is not appropriate here because SNP genotypes are static and lack meaningful temporal ordering; stratified random split is the correct choice for this data structure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Raw features consist of 500,000+ SNPs from the 1000 Genomes genotype calls. To reduce noise and dimensionality, we apply the following pipeline—fit exclusively on the training set, then applied to validation and test:
- **SNP filtering**: Remove SNPs with minor allele frequency (MAF) < 5% in the training set. This reduces computational burden and removes rare variants with insufficient training signal.
- **Imputation**: Impute missing genotypes using mean imputation, stratified by population and fit on training data only.
- **Encoding**: Encode genotypes as allele counts (0, 1, 2).
- **Normalization**: Apply z-score normalization (zero mean, unit variance) per SNP, with mean and std computed from the training set only.

Final feature dimensionality: ~50,000–100,000 SNPs. This high-dimensional, sparse feature regime (>1000× more features than samples) deliberately tests whether the transformer can avoid overfitting—a core design challenge.

### 4. Models and Baselines

**Transformer Model:** A bidirectional transformer encoder with 2–4 transformer blocks, 8 attention heads, hidden dimension 256, global pooling, and a 2-layer MLP classifier (hidden: 128, output: sigmoid). The model learns contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants. Regularization: dropout 0.3, L2 weight decay 1e-4, early stopping on validation AUC. Training: Adam optimizer, learning rate 1e-4 with cosine annealing, batch size 32, up to 100 epochs.

**Baseline: Logistic Regression** trained on the identical 50,000–100,000 SNP feature set (same preprocessing, fit on training only). The baseline is tuned with equivalent effort via grid search over regularization strength (C ∈ [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]), using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set to select the C that maximizes validation AUC. This is a fair comparison because both models use the same features, receive comparable hyperparameter optimization, and are evaluated identically. Logistic regression is the standard statistical approach in genomic prediction and represents current practice.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: AUC-ROC on the test set.** Justification: AUC-ROC is threshold-independent, appropriate for disease susceptibility prediction where clinical operating points may vary, and is standard in genomic literature. It captures the model's ability to rank positive cases higher than negatives—essential for stratifying patients into risk tiers.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Precision-Recall AUC (for high-recall clinical scenarios)
- Balanced accuracy, to ensure the model does not achieve high AUC by biasing toward one class
- Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. empirical frequency in test deciles)
- Per-population AUC, reported separately for each ancestry group to detect population-specific performance gaps

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed using the validation set (15% of data). The validation set is used once during baseline grid search and for early stopping during transformer training. The test set is held out and remains untouched until final evaluation. This prevents any information leakage from test to training decisions.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

**Population stratification:** Addressed by stratified random splits and per-population AUC reporting. By ensuring balanced ancestry representation, we prevent the model from learning ancestry-phenotype confounds rather than true genetic associations.

**Environmental/lifestyle factors:** The 1000 Genomes data provide limited environmental metadata. This design isolates genetic prediction; future work should incorporate environmental covariates if available.

**Overfitting:** Controlled via strong regularization (dropout, L2 penalty, early stopping) and validation monitoring. Training vs. validation AUC curves will be reported to detect divergence.

**Label noise:** Transparent label criteria and exclusion of ambiguous samples reduce noise. Sensitivity analyses will re-test the final model with relaxed thresholds.

### 8. Interpretation and Claims

This design will establish whether a transformer model can achieve higher test-set AUC than logistic regression on the same preprocessed feature set. Success would support the hypothesis that deep learning models capture non-linear SNP interactions relevant to disease susceptibility. Failure would suggest that linear models are sufficient for this task or that the feature engineering / regularization strategy requires revision. The experiment does not establish causality (prediction ≠ causal mechanism) and is limited to the ancestry populations represented in 1000 Genomes; generalization to other ancestry groups or environmental contexts requires separate validation.
